title: make pull-policy=newer finally working
body:
### Feature request description

Hi, 
several podman commands such as run and build have a parameter --pull-policy, where the man pages tell

       • newer: Pull if the image on the registry is newer than the one in the local containers storage.  An image is considered to be newer when the digests are different.  Comparing the time stamps is prone to errors.  Pull errors are suppressed if a local image was found.


Thousands of other program cans properly compare  times stamps. 

why the hell shouldn't podman be able to compare timestamps? That's standard function of runtime libraries, and it is just ridiculous to deny beeing able to do that. 

And I had to struggle with severe problems when podman replaced freshly built images with older ones. 

If you really need that, keep it as pull-policy=different, but do make pull-policy=newer working what it means, i.e. pull only newer images. 

It is not rocket science to compare two dates. 



### Suggest potential solution

A clear and concise description of what you want to happen.

### Have you considered any alternatives?

A clear and concise description of any alternative solutions or features you've considered.

### Additional context

Add any other context or screenshots about the feature request here.

comments.body:
Thanks for the report. The current behavior of --pull-policy=newer is intentional and documented - it compares manifest digests, not timestamps. From the [man page](https://github.com/podman-container-tools/podman/blob/main/docs/source/markdown/options/pull.md): 

> An image is considered to be newer when the digests are different. Comparing the time stamps is prone to errors.

The rationale: OCI image timestamps (Created field) are set by the builder and cannot be trusted. Digests, by contrast, are authoritative, a matching digest guarantees byte-for-byte identical content.

That said, I'd like to understand your actual workflow better, because your scenario suggests something going wrong.

What pull policy are you using when this happens? (Check podman info | grep -i pull or share the full command.)
If you want podman to prefer your locally built image and only pull when it's truly absent, --pull=missing is likely what you want. If you always want the registry version, use --pull=always.

I'm having a central (local, private) registry. Currently running just a plain „docker-registry“. 

Every now and then I do build new versions, and push them to this registry (and in future automatically with Gitea or Forgejo, on a weekly or monthly basis). 

And I do have a bunch of server and desktop machines, supposed to run the docker images, and pull them, if a newer version is available on the registry. 


But: 

Some of the machines are development machines, working on that particular docker images git code, and some of these machines (notebooks) are used on the road with slow or expensive (metered) connections. 

In some cases I do need to create/modify/update the docker images locally, e.g. when in a hotel. 

What I do need is:

1. automatically pull and update the image if there is a newer one.
2. but don't pull and update the image, if there is a newer one created locally on the machine, i.e. never do overwrite/delete a locally created image that is newer than the one on my central registry. 


Having the local process pull the image just because its hash is different from the local one, forces overwriting and thus deleting the local image. That's a no go. 

Because what I do need is the *latest* image, either from the private registry, or locally built. 

And *latest* means *newer*, not just *different*. 


And this is exactly, what the date is good for. That's the idea, after all.


And no, since I am the builder of my own docker images, I won't cheat my self with fake build dates. 



I do see that your argument is reasonable to some degree. But: 

1. What you are doing is something completely different than „newer“, and all documentation magic will not help you to make this behaviour appear as „newer“, because it just isn'. 

2. If you assume the attacker could fake the creation date, you're in much more trouble and more severe trouble and shouldn't use that image and image source at all. 

3. What if, as in my case, the user reliable knows the date can be trusted, and does need to just run the latest image, no matter whether from the private registry or locally built? After all, isn't that the user's own decision?

4. If you don't use the latest, and don't want to do that, why do you still call it "newer" then, which is just wrong by the definition of the term?

5. What's your proposed method to alway run the latest in terms of time and date?

6. Why is the default image tag 'latest' then?

Thanks for clarifying.

For your use case it would make sense to introduce a new option like `pull-policy=timestamp`. 

I don't see modifying the `newer` option until the next major release due to breaking change, but I could see a specific option that will check for the timestamp.
